content.text: The Azure Mobile App (available free on iOS and Android) lets you monitor Azure resources, respond to alerts, and run Cloud Shell commands from your phone. It does not replicate the full portal — it is optimised for on-call scenarios: checking resource health, starting or stopping VMs, reading and acknowledging alerts, and running quick CLI queries without needing a laptop.

attrs.alt: Azure Mobile App screens: Home with health alerts and pinned resources, Resource detail with metrics and Start/Stop/Restart actions, Cloud Shell terminal, and Alerts list by severity

content.text: The app is free and available on both major mobile platforms. It uses the same Microsoft account and Azure AD credentials as the portal.

# First-time setup:
1. Install the app from the App Store or Google Play
2. Open the app and tap "Sign in"
3. Sign in with your Microsoft account or work/school account
4. Complete MFA if required by your tenant's Conditional Access policy
5. The app prompts to select a directory if you have access to multiple tenants
6. The home screen shows your pinned resources and active alerts

# Switching accounts (multiple Azure AD tenants):
Settings (gear) > Accounts > Add account
- Add multiple accounts and switch between them without signing out
- Each account maintains its own pinned resources and notification settings
The home screen is your persistent monitoring view. You pin specific resources or dashboards to it so the most critical infrastructure is visible immediately when you open the app.
What Appears on the Home Screen
Service Health alerts: active incidents affecting Azure services in your regions appear at the top
Pinned resources: VMs, App Services, storage accounts, or any resource you pinned from the resource detail view
Recent activity: recent operations on your resources (same as the portal notification bell)
Quick links: shortcuts to Cloud Shell and the full resource list

# Pin a resource to the home screen:
1. In the app, tap the search icon or "Resources" tab
2. Find the resource (search by name or browse by type)
3. Open the resource detail page
4. Tap the star icon or the pin icon (top-right of the resource header)
5. The resource tile now appears on the home screen

# Reorder pinned resources:
Home screen > Edit (pencil icon) > drag tiles to reorder > Done

# Remove a pinned resource:
Home screen > Edit > tap the minus (-) on the tile > Done
OR
Resource detail page > tap the filled star/pin to unpin
The resource detail view in the mobile app shows the key properties, a live metrics chart (CPU, memory, requests), and the most common action buttons for that resource type.
Shuts down the OS; VM is stopped but not deallocated (still billed for compute)
Equivalent to a VM restart — OS reboots without deallocating
Opens a picker to change the VM SKU; requires VM stop
Opens RDP/SSH connection details (does not initiate the connection directly)

# Start a stopped VM from the mobile app:
1. Home screen > tap the VM tile (or search for it)
2. Resource detail page shows current state: "Stopped (deallocated)"
3. Tap "Start"
4. Confirm the action in the prompt
5. A progress notification appears; push notification fires when the VM is running

# Monitor startup progress:
- Stay on the resource detail page; the status updates automatically
- Or navigate away — you will receive a push notification when the VM reaches "Running"
- The notification includes the VM name, new state, and time elapsed

# Available actions for App Service in the mobile app:
- Start / Stop / Restart the app
- View application metrics: requests/sec, response time, failures
- View deployment slots and swap between them
- Browse to the app URL (opens in the mobile browser)

# Check App Service health from the mobile app:
1. Search or tap the pinned App Service
2. Metrics tab shows: Requests (30-min rolling), Avg response time, Failed requests
3. If Failed requests > 0, tap the metric to expand the chart
4. Activity log tab shows recent deployments and configuration changes
Azure Monitor Alerts in the Mobile App

The app receives push notifications for Azure Monitor alerts and displays them in the Alerts tab. This makes the mobile app the primary on-call tool for responding to infrastructure incidents without opening a laptop.

# Enable push notifications (one-time setup):
1. Open the Azure Mobile App
2. Settings (gear) > Notifications
3. Toggle "Push notifications" to ON
4. Allow the OS-level notification permission when prompted

# Notifications are sent for:
- Azure Monitor metric alerts (CPU, memory, disk, network)
- Azure Monitor log alerts (custom KQL queries)
- Activity log alerts (resource deleted, RBAC changed, policy violated)
- Service Health alerts (Azure outages and maintenance)
- Budget alerts (spending threshold crossed)

# To stop receiving notifications for a specific alert rule:
Option 1: Disable the alert rule in Azure Monitor
Option 2: Remove the mobile app from the action group's target list
Configuring an Action Group for Mobile Notifications

# Create an action group that sends push notifications to the Azure Mobile App
az monitor action-group create \
  --name ag-mobile-oncall \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --short-name mobile \
  --azure-app-push-receiver \
    name=oncall-engineer \
    email-address=alice@contoso.com

# Add the action group to a metric alert rule
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --name alert-cpu-high \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-prod-01" \
  --condition "avg Percentage CPU > 85" \
  --window-size 5m \
  --evaluation-frequency 1m \
  --severity 2 \
  --action ag-mobile-oncall \
  --description "CPU above 85% for 5 minutes"

echo "Alert rule created — push notifications will go to alice@contoso.com mobile app"
Cloud Shell in the Mobile App

Cloud Shell is embedded in the Azure Mobile App with full Bash and PowerShell support. It is pre-authenticated to your subscriptions — the same session as the web portal Cloud Shell, sharing the same persistent storage mount (clouddrive).

# Common on-call tasks runnable from mobile Cloud Shell:

# Check which VMs are stopped
az vm list \
  --query "[?powerState=='VM stopped'].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup}" \
  -o table

# Restart a web app
az webapp restart \
  --name app-prod \
  --resource-group rg-prod

# Check recent failed operations
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --status Failed \
  --max-events 10 \
  -o table

# Scale out an App Service plan
az appservice plan update \
  --name asp-prod \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --number-of-workers 4

# Check storage account availability
az storage account show \
  --name stprod001 \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "statusOfPrimary" \
  -o tsv
Use the globe icon in the toolbar to switch between Bash and PowerShell
The up-arrow key recalls command history — critical on a mobile keyboard
Tap and hold on output to copy selected text
Portrait mode gives a narrow terminal; landscape mode is much more usable for Cloud Shell
Files saved to /home/<user>/clouddrive persist across sessions (stored in Azure Files)
Session times out after 20 minutes of inactivity; the app reconnects automatically when you return
Service Health in the Mobile App

The Service Health section shows active Azure outages, degraded services, planned maintenance, and health advisories. On mobile, this is the fastest way to check whether an alert is caused by your infrastructure or by an Azure platform issue.

# Navigate to Service Health in the mobile app:
Home screen > Service Health (if alerts are showing)
OR
Resources tab > search "Service Health" > tap the Service Health blade

# Service Health screens:
Service Issues    — current outages and degraded services by region and service type
Planned maintenance — upcoming maintenance windows that may affect your resources
Health advisories — recommendations and retirement notices
Health history   — past incidents with root cause analysis (RCA) documents

# When an alert fires and you suspect a platform issue:
1. Open Service Health
2. Filter by your resource's region (e.g., East US)
3. Check if the affected service type is listed as degraded
4. If yes: no action needed on your end; wait for Azure to resolve
5. If no: investigate your own resource (logs, metrics, configuration)
Mobile App vs Portal vs CLI — When to Use Each
On-Call Runbook: Responding to Alerts via Mobile

This section describes a complete on-call response workflow using only the Azure Mobile App. Follow this pattern when an alert push notification wakes you at 3 AM and you need to triage and remediate without opening a laptop.

# When you receive a push notification:
1. Tap the notification to open the alert detail in the app
2. Note: severity (Sev 1–4), resource name, metric/condition, fired time
3. Check Service Health:
   App home > Service Health icon
   → Is Azure reporting an outage in your resource's region?
   → If YES: track the incident; no action on your resources until Azure resolves
   → If NO: the issue is in your infrastructure; continue triage

# Check resource status:
4. Navigate to the resource (tap its name in the alert detail)
5. Review the Metrics tab:
   - Is the metric still elevated or already recovering?
   - Compare current value to the threshold in the alert
6. Check Activity log:
   - Was there a recent deployment or config change before the alert fired?

# High CPU — restart the process without deallocating:
# Mobile app: Resource detail > Restart
# Or in Cloud Shell:
az webapp restart \
  --name app-prod \
  --resource-group rg-prod

# High CPU on VM — check top processes via Cloud Shell:
az vm run-command invoke \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --command-id RunShellScript \
  --scripts "ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -10"

# Memory pressure — scale out (add instances):
az appservice plan update \
  --name asp-prod \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --number-of-workers 3

# Disk full on VM — identify large files:
az vm run-command invoke \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --command-id RunShellScript \
  --scripts "df -h && du -sh /var/log/* | sort -h | tail -10"

# Stop a runaway process on a VM:
az vm run-command invoke \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --command-id RunShellScript \
  --scripts "systemctl restart myservice"

# Verify the metric is back within normal range:
# Mobile app: Resource detail > Metrics tab — watch for 5 minutes
# Or in Cloud Shell:
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-prod-01" \
  --metric "Percentage CPU" \
  --interval PT1M \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '30 minutes ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --aggregation Average \
  --query "value[].timeseries[].data[].[timeStamp,average]" \
  -o table

# Check if the alert auto-resolved:
# Mobile app: Alerts tab — alert state changes to "Resolved"
# Or via CLI:
az monitor metrics alert show \
  --name alert-cpu-high \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "{State:properties.enabled, LastFired:properties.lastModifiedUtc}" \
  -o table
Granting mobile app access to production Azure subscriptions is a conscious security decision. Apply the following controls to reduce the risk of a compromised phone leading to a production incident.
Require MFA for mobile app login
Conditional Access policy targeting all apps for mobile device platforms
Prevents account takeover if phone is stolen without the authenticator app
Intune device compliance policy + CA conditional access
Blocks sign-in from unencrypted or jailbroken phones
CA policy: require re-authentication every 8 hours
Limits how long a stolen session token is valid
Limit mobile app to specific roles
CA policy: target the Azure Management app (ID: 797f4846...)
Restricts which users/roles can access Azure via the mobile app
App Settings > App lock > Enable PIN/Face/Fingerprint
Requires additional authentication within the app even with a signed-in session

The Azure Mobile App supports multiple Microsoft accounts simultaneously. Engineers who manage multiple customer tenants (via CSP or Azure Lighthouse) can add each account and switch between them without signing out.

# Add multiple accounts in the mobile app:
Settings (gear) > Accounts > + Add account
1. Sign in with the second Microsoft account or work account
2. Complete MFA if required
3. The app adds the account to the account switcher

# Switch between accounts:
Settings (gear) > Accounts > tap the account to switch to
OR
Tap your avatar/initials in the top corner > select account

# Switch between directories (multiple tenants in one account):
Settings (gear) > Directory > select the tenant
OR
Tap your avatar > switch directory

# Each account/directory has its own:
- Pinned resources (separate home screen per directory)
- Alert notifications
- Cloud Shell environment and clouddrive storage
- Recent resources history

If you manage a large number of customer tenants via Azure Lighthouse, use the Delegated Resources view in the portal (not the mobile app) for a cross-tenant resource list. The mobile app shows resources scoped to the active directory — you would need to switch directories for each customer.

The Azure Mobile App is a free iOS and Android application for on-call monitoring and quick remediation. Pin your critical resources to the home screen for instant status checks. The app delivers Azure Monitor alert push notifications and lets you start, stop, and restart resources with a single tap. Cloud Shell is fully available in the app for running CLI commands without a laptop. For diagnosing alerts, use Service Health to rule out platform-level Azure issues before investigating your own infrastructure. The mobile app is not a replacement for the portal or CLI — it is purpose-built for reactive, on-call use cases where response speed matters more than breadth of functionality.
